1: Hellēnistai pros tous Hebraious ('Hellenists against the Hebrews'): the two groups are Greek-speaking diaspora Jews who had settled in Jerusalem versus Aramaic-speaking native Palestinian Jews. Both were Christians; the division reflects the sociolinguistic fault line within Second Temple Judaism rather than any ethnic distinction. The complaint about the daily food distribution (en tē diakonia tē kathēmerinē) identifies the church's first internal administrative failure — not a theological dispute but a logistical inequity with demographic dimensions.
2: kataleipsantas ton logon tou theou ('leaving/neglecting the word of God'): the Twelve's reasoning prioritizes the ministry of the word over administrative management. diakonein trapezais ('serve tables'): diakonia (service/ministry) will be applied both to the Twelve's ministry of the word (Acts 6:4) and to the Seven's table service — Luke does not demean the Seven's work but establishes a differentiation of function. Both are forms of diakonia.
3: martyroumenous (martyroumenous, 'well-attested/of good reputation'): the qualifications for the Seven — good reputation, Spirit-filling, wisdom — are the same qualities required for leadership throughout the Pastoral Epistles and point to the proto-diaconal office. Plēreis pneumatos hagiou kai sophias ('full of the Holy Spirit and wisdom'): the pairing of Spirit and wisdom reflects the OT endowment of the craftsmen of the Tabernacle (Exodus 31:3; Acts 35:31) and the Servant (Isaiah 11:2), marking these men as set apart for divine service.
4: proskarterēsomen (proskarterēsomen, 'we will devote ourselves'): the same verb used for the community's prayer in 1:14 and for their adherence to the apostles' teaching in Acts 2:42. The Twelve's commitment to prayer and the word-ministry is not a demotion of administrative work but a recognition of their specific calling. The word-and-prayer combination is the apostolic core from which everything else flows.
5: Stephanos, anēr plērēs pisteōs kai pneumatos hagiou ('Stephen, a man full of faith and the Holy Spirit'): Luke's introduction of Stephen singles him out from the Seven with the same Spirit-fullness language that will characterize his subsequent ministry (Acts 6:8) and his death (Acts 7:55). The Seven's names are all Greek (Nikolaos alone is explicitly identified as a proselyte), suggesting that the seven chosen to serve the Hellenist widows were themselves Hellenist Jews — an administratively elegant solution.
6: proseuxamenoi epethēkan autois tas cheiras ('having prayed they laid their hands on them'): the commissioning gesture combines prayer and handlaying. In the OT, handlaying transferred authority and blessing (Numbers 27:18-23, Joshua's commissioning; Deuteronomy 34:9, the Spirit of wisdom transferred to Joshua). Here it is not ordination in a later technical sense but a formal recognition and authorization of the Seven's specific task. The same gesture in 13:3 will commission Paul and Barnabas for the Gentile mission.
7: ho logos tou theou ēuxanen ('the word of God kept growing'): the third of Luke's Acts progress reports (Acts 2:47; Acts 4:4; Acts 5:14; Acts 6:7 — and more to follow). Each summary follows resolution of a crisis: the Ananias crisis resolved → growth; the Sanhedrin crisis resolved → growth; the Hellenist administrative crisis resolved → growth. polys te ochlos tōn hiereōn hypēkouon tē pistei ('a large number of priests were obedient to the faith'): this detail is striking — members of the temple establishment, the class most implicated in Jesus's crucifixion, are being converted. The gospel is reaching the center of official Judaism.
8: plērēs charitos kai dynameōs ('full of grace and power'): Stephen's characterization shifts from administrative appointment to apostolic-level ministry — sēmeia kai terata megala ('great signs and wonders'). The same vocabulary used for the apostles (Acts 2:43; Acts 5:12) is now applied to one of the Seven. Luke signals that the Spirit's work through the Hellenist leaders extends beyond the Twelve, preparing for the geographic expansion of the mission through Stephen's speech and Philip's ministry in Acts 8.
9: Synagōgē Libertinōn ('Synagogue of the Freedmen'): likely descendants of Jews taken to Rome as slaves by Pompey in 63 BC and subsequently freed, who maintained their own synagogue in Jerusalem. The listing of multiple groups — Cyrenians, Alexandrians, Cilicians, Asians — indicates that Stephen's opponents were Diaspora Jews who had returned to Jerusalem, many of them Greek-speakers like Stephen himself. The opposition comes from within the Hellenist community, not from native Palestinians.
10: ouk ischyon antistēnai tē sophia kai tō pneumati ('they could not withstand the wisdom and the Spirit'): the opposition's inability to overcome Stephen's arguments fulfills Jesus's promise of Luke 21:15 ('I will give you a mouth and wisdom which none of your adversaries will be able to withstand or contradict'). Sophia (wisdom) and pneuma (Spirit) are the gifts that cannot be matched by the synagogue debaters' conventional learning.
11: blasphēmous logous eis Mōusēn kai ton theon ('blasphemous words against Moses and God'): the charge of blasphemy — the technical capital offence under Jewish law — is introduced through suborned false witnesses (hypebalan andras, 'they secretly persuaded men'). Luke's language explicitly marks the testimony as false (martyras pseudeis, Acts 6:13), paralleling Jesus's trial (Luke 22:66-71). Stephen's situation replicates his Lord's: falsely accused of blasphemy before the same body.
12: kinēsantes ton laon kai tous presbyterous kai tous grammateis ('stirring up the people and the elders and the scribes'): the progression is significant — the opposition cannot refute Stephen's arguments (Acts 6:10), so they resort to crowd agitation and official mobilization. The laos (people) who served as protection for the apostles in Acts 4-5 is now being turned against Stephen. The populace is more volatile than the Sanhedrin's institutional opposition.
13: martyras pseudeis ('false witnesses'): Luke's explicit identification of the witnesses as false contrasts with the apostolic martyria (true witness). The parallel to Jesus's trial is precise: false witnesses at the Sanhedrin, the charge of threatening the temple, the high priest's questioning. Luke is constructing a deliberate parallel between Stephen's passion and Jesus's passion, preparing the reader to understand Stephen as the proto-martyr who follows his Lord's pattern fully.
14: katalysei ton topon touton ('will destroy this place'): the charge echoes the accusation at Jesus's trial (Mark 14:58; Matthew 26:61). Stephen's actual theology of the temple — worked out in Acts 7:44-50 — argues not for destruction but for transcendence: the temple was always a pointer to something greater that has now arrived. The accusation distorts his position. allaxei ta ethē ('will change the customs'): this is the deeper charge — that Jesus changes what Moses established — which Acts 7 addresses as Torah correctly understood pointing to Christ.
15: hōsei prosōpon angelou ('like the face of an angel'): Stephen's transfigured appearance before the Sanhedrin prolepticly manifests the glory he will see in Acts 6:7:55-56. In the OT, the face-of-an-angel motif marks someone as a divine messenger in the presence of God (cf. Daniel 3:25, 'the fourth man is like a son of the gods'; Judges 13:6, 'the man of God had the face of an angel'). The Sanhedrin is not simply judging a man; they are, unknowingly, facing a man who is standing at the threshold of the divine glory he is about to fully enter.
